content.text: are the two other core capabilities servers can expose alongside Tools. Together, they form the complete MCP primitive set:
the model can read (like files, database records, logs, images).
Safe to read multiple times without side effects (like GET endpoint)
Give LLM information too large/dynamic for system prompt
Each resource has a standardized definition:
: Server defines what each scheme means and how to resolve it
"Recent application logs from the last hour"
"Database schema for users table with columns: id, name, email"
"Configuration file with API keys and endpoints"
: Client knows how to handle/render content

sequenceDiagram
    Client->>Server: resources/list
    Server->>Client: [resource defs with URIs]
    Client->>Model: Inject resource list
    Model->>Client: Decide to read resource
    Client->>Server: resources/read (uri)
    Server->>Client: {content: "text data"}
When the model wants to read a resource:
Server resolves the URI and returns content:

:
- Extracts function name → resource name
- Extracts docstring → description
- Generates URI pattern from decorator
- Registers with
: User reports issue → LLM reads logs → diagnoses problem
: LLM needs to write SQL queries → reads schema → understands columns
: LLM needs API credentials → reads config → makes requests
When to Use Resources vs Tools
that inject structured instructions into the conversation.
Reusable instruction sets with parameterized values
Can inject system instructions + conversation history
Host apps show them as triggerable commands
that fill in parameters at use time.

sequenceDiagram
    Client->>Server: prompts/list
    Server->>Client: [prompt templates]
    Client->>UI: Show as slash commands
    User->>UI: Type "/write_blog_post"
    UI->>Client: Ask for parameters
    Client->>Server: prompts/get (name, args)
    Server->>Client: {messages: [...]}
    Client->>Model: Inject into conversation

from mcp.server import Server

app = Server("prompt-server")

@app.prompt()
def write_blog_post(topic: str, style: str = "casual") -> str:
    """Generate a blog post with a specific style.
    
    Args:
        topic: The blog post topic (e.g., 'AI', 'Machine Learning')
        style: Writing style (casual, formal, technical)
    """
    return f"""Write a {style} blog post about {topic}.
    
Requirements:
- Include real-world examples
- Explain technical concepts clearly
- End with a conclusion"""
→ fills in parameters → prompt injects multi-turn context
Why Prompts Are Rarely Used (But Powerful)

:
- ❌ Less obvious than tools (no "execute" action)
- ❌ Require understanding of conversation injection
- ❌ Tutorial gap (few examples)
: Large context, dynamic data, file attachments
: Standardize workflows, reduce token waste, enforce best practices
